title: Guitar Basics: Parts & Strings
description: Learn the basic parts of a guitar and understand the strings

questions.question: How many strings does a standard acoustic guitar have?
questions.explanation: A standard acoustic guitar has 6 strings tuned to E-A-D-G-B-E from lowest to highest.

questions.question: What is the head of the guitar?
options: The part at the end of the neck with tuning pegs
questions.correctAnswer: The part at the end of the neck with tuning pegs
questions.explanation: The headstock (or head) is located at the end of the guitar neck and holds the tuning pegs.

questions.question: True or False: The fretboard is where you press your fingers to change the notes.
questions.explanation: The fretboard contains metal strips called frets that divide the neck into sections. Pressing strings against different frets changes the pitch.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a part of a guitar?
questions.explanation: A piston is not a part of a guitar. Soundhole, pickguard, and bridge are all essential parts.

questions.question: What does the bridge do on a guitar?
questions.explanation: The bridge anchors the strings on the body of the guitar and transfers string vibrations to the soundboard.

title: Guitar Basics: Open Chords
description: Master the fundamental open chords every guitarist should know

title: Guitar Beginner Essentials
description: Basic tuning, open chords, and simple rhythm for guitar.

questions.question: Which of these is the standard tuning for a 6-string guitar from lowest to highest?
options: E A D G B E
A D G C E A
D G C F A D
E B G D A E
questions.correctAnswer: E A D G B E
questions.explanation: Standard tuning for a 6-string guitar is E-A-D-G-B-E.

questions.question: True or False: An open chord includes at least one open string.
questions.explanation: An open chord uses one or more strings that are played without fretting.

questions.question: Which chord is an example of an open major chord?
questions.explanation: C Major is a common open chord played on acoustic guitar.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the guitar pick?
options: To pluck or strum the strings
questions.correctAnswer: To pluck or strum the strings
questions.explanation: A pick is used to strike or pluck the strings to produce sound.

title: Guitar Intermediate: Barre & Rhythm
description: Barre chords, rhythm patterns, and scale basics.

questions.question: What does a barre chord allow you to do?
options: Use one finger to press multiple strings
questions.correctAnswer: Use one finger to press multiple strings
questions.explanation: A barre chord uses one finger to press multiple strings across a fret.

questions.question: Which scale is commonly used for rock and blues improvisation?
questions.explanation: The minor pentatonic scale is widely used in rock and blues solos.

questions.question: True or False: Palm muting produces a more muted, percussive sound.
questions.explanation: Palm muting dampens the strings with the picking hand to create a muted sound.

questions.question: Which technique helps with fast alternate-note runs?
questions.explanation: Alternate picking uses alternating down and up strokes for speed and precision.

title: Guitar Advanced Techniques
description: Tapping, sweep picking, hybrid picking and advanced phrasing.

questions.question: Which technique uses the picking hand to fret notes?
questions.explanation: Tapping uses the picking hand to press notes onto the fretboard.

questions.question: What is sweep picking mainly used for?
questions.explanation: Sweep picking allows fast, smooth arpeggio execution across strings.

questions.question: True or False: Hybrid picking combines a pick and fingers to pluck strings.
questions.explanation: Hybrid picking uses a pick plus fingers for complex phrasing.

questions.question: Which technique adds percussive attack by slapping the strings?
questions.explanation: Slap technique strikes strings with the thumb for percussive effect.

title: Piano Beginner Essentials
description: Notes, octave layout, and basic finger numbers.

questions.question: How many keys are on a standard piano?
questions.explanation: A standard piano has 88 keys from A0 to C8.

questions.question: True or False: Middle C is near the center of the keyboard.
questions.explanation: Middle C (C4) is approximately in the middle of an 88-key keyboard.

questions.question: How many black keys are in one octave?
questions.explanation: Each octave has 5 black keys and 7 white keys.

questions.question: Which finger number is commonly used for the thumb?
questions.explanation: In piano fingering, the thumb is numbered 1.

title: Piano Intermediate: Scales & Chords
description: Major/minor scales, basic arpeggios, and chord shapes.

questions.question: How many notes are in a diatonic major scale?
questions.explanation: A major scale contains seven distinct notes before the octave.

questions.question: What is an arpeggio?
options: A chord played one note at a time
questions.correctAnswer: A chord played one note at a time
questions.explanation: An arpeggio breaks a chord into separate notes played sequentially.

questions.question: True or False: Inversions change the order of notes in a chord.
questions.explanation: Inversions reorder chord notes so a different note is in the bass.

questions.question: Which chord consists of the notes C-E-G?
questions.explanation: C major triad is C-E-G.

title: Piano Advanced Harmony
description: Extended chords, voicings, and modulation basics.

questions.question: Which progression is central to jazz harmony?
questions.explanation: The ii-V-I progression is fundamental in jazz harmony.

questions.question: True or False: Voice leading aims to move each part smoothly between chords.
questions.explanation: Voice leading smooths individual melodic lines between chords.

questions.question: What does a suspended chord replace in a triad?
questions.explanation: A suspended chord replaces the third with either the second or fourth.

questions.question: Which extended chord includes the 7th and 9th?
questions.explanation: A ninth chord stacks a seventh plus an added ninth above a triad.

title: Drums Beginner Basics
description: Names of drums, basic stick grip, and simple beats.

questions.question: Which drum is played with a foot pedal?
questions.explanation: The bass drum is operated with a foot pedal.

questions.question: True or False: The snare drum typically has metal wires called snares beneath it.
questions.explanation: Snares on the bottom head produce the snare drum's distinctive sound.

questions.question: What is the common time signature for basic rock beats?
questions.explanation: Most basic rock beats are in 4/4 time.

questions.question: Which cymbal is often used for steady timekeeping?
questions.explanation: The ride cymbal provides a steady rhythmic pattern in many styles.

title: Drums Intermediate: Grooves & Fills
description: Paradiddles, coordination, and simple fills.

questions.question: What is a paradiddle?
questions.explanation: A paradiddle is a sticking pattern (RLRR LRLL) used for coordination.

questions.question: True or False: A drum fill typically helps transition between song sections.
questions.explanation: Fills are used to lead into new sections or change the feel.

questions.question: Which limb independence target is common for intermediate players?
options: Playing different patterns with hands and feet
questions.correctAnswer: Playing different patterns with hands and feet
questions.explanation: Limb independence trains hands and feet to play complementary rhythms.

questions.question: Which dynamic indicates very soft playing?
questions.explanation: pp stands for pianissimo, meaning very soft.

title: Drums Advanced: Polyrhythms & Odd Time
description: Polyrhythms, odd meters, and advanced fills.

questions.question: What is a polyrhythm?
questions.explanation: Polyrhythms layer different rhythmic groupings simultaneously.

questions.question: Which meter is an example of odd time?
questions.explanation: 7/8 is an odd meter that groups beats unevenly.

questions.question: True or False: A backbeat usually emphasizes beats 2 and 4 in 4/4.
questions.explanation: The backbeat accentuates beats 2 and 4, common in many popular styles.

questions.question: Which technique uses brushes instead of sticks for a softer sound?
questions.explanation: Brushes create a softer texture and are commonly used in jazz.

title: Violin Beginner: Parts & Tuning
description: Basic parts, strings, and simple posture.

questions.question: How many strings does a standard violin have?
questions.explanation: A standard violin has 4 strings tuned G-D-A-E.

questions.question: True or False: The bow is drawn across the strings to produce sound.
questions.explanation: The bow's horsehair vibrates the strings to create sound.

questions.question: What is the purpose of rosin on the bow?
questions.explanation: Rosin increases grip between bow hair and strings for sound production.

questions.question: Which string is the highest pitch on the violin?
questions.explanation: The E string is the highest pitch on a standard violin.

title: Violin Intermediate: Shifting & Vibrato
description: Shifting positions, vibrato basics, and bow control.

questions.question: What does shifting allow a violinist to do?
questions.explanation: Shifting moves the left hand to higher positions to access higher pitches.

questions.question: True or False: Vibrato adds warmth and expression to sustained notes.
questions.explanation: Vibrato varies pitch slightly to add expression to notes.

questions.question: Which bow stroke produces a smooth connected sound?
questions.explanation: Legato creates smooth, connected lines when bowed.

questions.question: What is a double stop?
questions.explanation: A double stop is when two strings are played at the same time.

title: Violin Advanced: Double Stops & Bowing
description: Double stops, advanced bow strokes, and performance techniques.

questions.question: Which stroke creates a short bouncing sound?
questions.explanation: Spiccato is a bouncing bow stroke that produces short detached notes.

questions.question: True or False: Double stops require shifting and careful intonation.
questions.explanation: Double stops require precise finger placement and intonation.

questions.question: What is pizzicato?
questions.explanation: Pizzicato is played by plucking the strings with the finger.

questions.question: Which practice improves left-hand intonation?
questions.explanation: Practicing slow scales against a drone helps improve intonation.

title: Flute Beginner: Posture & Tone
description: Posture, embouchure, and producing first tones.

questions.question: Where do you blow to produce sound on a flute?
questions.explanation: Flute sound is produced by blowing across the embouchure hole on the headjoint.

questions.question: True or False: The flute is held horizontally when playing.
questions.explanation: The flute is held to the right side of the player and played horizontally.

questions.question: Which part of the flute contains the embouchure hole?
questions.explanation: The embouchure hole is located on the headjoint of the flute.

questions.question: What helps produce a clear tone?
questions.explanation: A steady airstream is essential for a clean flute tone.

title: Flute Intermediate: Fingerings & Articulation
description: Intermediate fingerings, articulation, and tone control.

questions.question: What improves tone quality on flute?
questions.explanation: Consistent breath support helps produce a stable tone.

questions.question: True or False: Double tonguing is used for fast repeated notes.
questions.explanation: Double tonguing uses alternating syllables for rapid articulation.

questions.question: Which articulation produces short detached notes?
questions.explanation: Staccato produces short, detached notes.

questions.question: Which register is above the normal range?
questions.explanation: Altissimo refers to the extended high register on the flute.

title: Flute Advanced: Altissimo & Expression
description: Advanced altissimo, extended technique, and expression.

questions.question: What does altissimo refer to?
questions.explanation: Altissimo refers to very high notes above the normal range.

questions.question: True or False: Extended techniques include multiphonics and flutter tonguing.
questions.explanation: Extended techniques include multiphonics and flutter tonguing among others.

questions.question: Which practice helps control high register notes?
questions.explanation: Strong, focused breath support aids high register control.

questions.question: Which articulation is useful for jazz phrasing?
questions.explanation: Flexible articulation helps shape jazz phrasing and expression.

title: Saxophone Beginner: Setup & Sound
description: Assembling, reed basics, and producing first sounds.

questions.question: Which part holds the reed on a saxophone?
questions.explanation: The reed is attached to the mouthpiece to produce sound.

questions.question: True or False: A softer reed is easier for beginners.
questions.explanation: Softer reeds require less embouchure strength and are easier for beginners.

questions.question: Which key helps reach higher notes?
questions.explanation: The octave key allows access to the upper register.

questions.question: What is important for a steady tone?
questions.explanation: Consistent breath support helps produce a steady tone.

title: Saxophone Intermediate: Tone & Technique
description: Tone production, breath, and basic jazz phrasing.

questions.question: Which practice improves consistent tone?
questions.explanation: Long tone practice and breath support improve tone consistency.

questions.question: True or False: The octave key helps play higher notes.
questions.explanation: The octave key assists in producing higher register notes.

questions.question: Which technique aids jazz phrasing?
questions.explanation: A flexible swing feel is central to jazz phrasing.

questions.question: Which articulation enhances clarity?
questions.explanation: Good tonguing technique helps articulate notes clearly.

title: Saxophone Advanced: Jazz & Altissimo
description: Advanced jazz phrasing, altissimo, and expression.

questions.question: What style commonly uses swing phrasing?
questions.explanation: Swing phrasing is a hallmark of jazz style.

questions.question: True or False: Altissimo requires specialized fingerings and embouchure.
questions.explanation: Altissimo uses specialized techniques for very high notes.

questions.question: Which element is key for expressive solos?
questions.explanation: Phrasing and dynamics are crucial for expressive solos.

questions.question: Which practice helps reach altissimo notes?
questions.explanation: Targeted embouchure and harmonic practice help access altissimo.

title: Trumpet Beginner: Buzz & Hold
description: Buzzing, basic fingering, and holding the instrument.

questions.question: What starts the trumpet sound?
questions.explanation: Trumpet sound begins from the vibration of the player's lips against the mouthpiece.

questions.question: True or False: The trumpet is supported by the left hand while valves are pressed with the right.
questions.explanation: The left hand supports the trumpet while the right operates the valves.

questions.question: Which part changes pitch on the trumpet?
questions.explanation: Valves change the length of the tubing and alter pitch.

questions.question: What helps produce higher notes?
questions.explanation: Strong, focused breath support aids higher register notes.

title: Trumpet Intermediate: Range & Articulation
description: Valve combinations, articulation, and range work.

questions.question: Which valve combination often plays low G?
questions.explanation: First and second valves together produce lower pitches like low G.

questions.question: True or False: Smooth tonguing is important for lyrical trumpet lines.
questions.explanation: Clean tonguing helps make lyrical trumpet lines smooth and musical.

questions.question: Which practice develops mid-range control?
questions.explanation: Lip slurs and long-tone practice improve control across the range.

questions.question: What does mute change?
questions.explanation: Mutes change the tone color and reduce volume.

title: Trumpet Advanced: Upper Register & Flexibility
description: High register control, flexibility, and orchestral techniques.

questions.question: Which is essential for strong upper register playing?
questions.explanation: Firm breath support and embouchure control are essential for the upper register.

questions.question: True or False: Flexibility exercises improve range and endurance.
questions.explanation: Flexibility exercises like lip slurs improve range and endurance.

questions.question: What helps produce smooth slurs?
questions.explanation: Legato tonguing combined with steady airflow produces smooth slurs.

questions.question: Which piece of equipment affects tone color most?
questions.explanation: Mouthpiece shape and cup depth influence tone color significantly.

title: Bass Beginner: Strings & Groove
description: Open strings, plucking technique, and basic grooves.

questions.question: What are the standard open strings on a 4-string bass from lowest to highest?
questions.explanation: Standard 4-string bass tuning is E-A-D-G from lowest to highest.

questions.question: True or False: Alternating index and middle fingers is a common plucking technique.
questions.explanation: Alternating index and middle fingers is common for basic bass lines.

questions.question: Which role does bass usually serve in a band?
questions.explanation: Bass provides both rhythm and harmonic support in many genres.

questions.question: Which tool helps tune a bass?
questions.explanation: A tuner helps ensure the instrument is in tune.

title: Bass Intermediate: Groove & Walking
description: Walking bass lines, locking with drums, and tone control.

questions.question: What does a walking bass line usually use?
questions.explanation: Walking bass lines move between chord tones using passing notes.

questions.question: True or False: Locking in with the drummer helps keep the groove tight.
questions.explanation: Bass and drums working together create a solid rhythmic foundation.

questions.question: Which technique adds percussive attack on bass?
questions.explanation: Slap uses the thumb to strike strings producing a percussive sound.

questions.question: Which practice improves timing?
questions.explanation: Practicing with a metronome improves timing and consistency.

title: Bass Advanced: Slap & Soloing
description: Slap technique, tapping, and melodic soloing.

questions.question: What is slap bass?
questions.explanation: Slap bass uses the thumb to strike the strings for a percussive attack.

questions.question: True or False: Melodic bass solos rely on phrasing and scale choices.
questions.explanation: Good bass solos use phrasing and appropriate scales rather than random notes.

questions.question: Which effect can thicken bass tone?
questions.explanation: Compression evens out dynamics and can make bass tone thicker.

questions.question: Which technique adds harmonic color?
questions.explanation: Natural and artificial harmonics add distinct tonal colors.
